entry.title: Unable to filter by a temporal ATTR in a DataSphere's AM
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

Has anyone experienced issues when filtering by an employee’s temporal attribute in an Analytical Model in Datasphere?

The issue occurs regardless of whether a reference date is used. It seems that the Analytical Model first applies the reference date filter and then the prompt filter, which prevents me from adding Year and Month prompts.

Has anyone faced a similar behavior or found a workaround?

Thanks in advance!

entry.title: Re: Execute LI20 in inventory stock
Re: SAP Managed Space (Ingestion)
entry.content:
Venky, it's correct in both cases, SAP HANA Database (Disk and In-Memory).

First, Foundation Services (FOS) layer stores the "Bronze/Silver/Gold" versions of data products in HANA Data Lake Files (HDLF), but then the data must be surfaced in SAP Datasphere for modeling and consumption.

Datasphere Ingestion Space ( in your caseSAP_S4H), The visible space inside your Datasphere tenant often utilizes the SAP HANA Database storage type for this specific space to ensure high-performance access for analytical modeling.

entry.title: Re: iOS app signing error and other error message for deprecated instructions
entry.content:
Hi Chantal,

My bad, the issue seem to be coming from `fastlane`, older version works fine, but if you are using the latest you need to add `--verbose`
so the final command should look like this:
```
xcrun actool "$HOME/Downloads/self-sign/Assets.xcassets" --compile "$HOME/Downloads/self-sign/ipa/Payload/RNVApp.app" --platform iphoneos --minimum-deployment-target 15.5 --app-icon AppIcon --output-partial-info-plist "$HOME/Downloads/self-sign/partial.plist"
(cd "$HOME/Downloads/self-sign/ipa" && zip -r ../../app.ipa Payload)
fastlane run resign --verbose ipa:"$HOME/Downloads/app.ipa" entitlements:"$HOME/Downloads/self-sign/ipa/Payload/RNVApp.app/app.entitlements" provisioning_profile:"$HOME/Downloads/self-sign/$provisioning_profile" signing_identity:"$signing_identity" bundle_id:"$bundle_id" display_name:"$display_name" short_version:"$short_version" bundle_version:"$bundle_version"
```
https://github.com/fastlane/fastlane/issues/29903

entry.title: 🚀 SAP Job Scheduling Service: Free Tier Now Available on BTP Live
entry.content:
🚀 Free Tier Now Available on BTP Live: Schedule Jobs at Zero Cost!

Great news for the SAP BTP community! After the successful launch of our Free plan on BTP Trial, we're thrilled to announce that the Free tier for SAP Job Scheduling Service is now available on BTP Live (Production)! 🎉

Whether you're building personal projects, creating proof-of-concepts, or running small-scale production workloads, you can now leverage the power of automated job scheduling without incurring any costs!

🌟 What is the Free Tier?

The Free tier is a no-cost service plan that provides you with production-grade job scheduling capabilities on SAP Business Technology Platform. It's designed to give developers, students, indie builders, and enterprises access to powerful scheduling features without financial barriers.

Key Highlights

✅ Zero Cost - No charges, no hidden fees, no credit card required.
✅ Production Ready - Available on both BTP Trial AND BTP Live.
✅ OAuth 2.0 Security - Modern authentication, same as Standard plan.
✅ Full Feature Set - Access to all core scheduling capabilities.
✅ 15 Schedules - Enough for real-world small to medium applications.
✅ Hourly Scheduling - Perfect for regular tasks and automation.

🎯 Who is the Free Tier For?

The Free tier is perfect for:

🧪 Proof of Concepts

  • Validate scheduling logic before committing to Standard plan
  • Demonstrate capabilities to stakeholders
  • Test integration with other BTP services
  • Build MVPs and demos

🏢 Small Production Workloads

  • Run lightweight scheduled tasks in production
  • Automate periodic maintenance jobs
  • Schedule regular reports and notifications
  • Power small-scale applications

💼 Enterprise Evaluation

  • Test Job Scheduling Service in your landscape
  • Evaluate before enterprise-wide adoption
  • Train your team on production patterns
  • Prototype solutions for larger implementations

Update to the Standard plan when you need more schedules, sub-hourly intervals, or SLA-backed support for business-critical workloads.

🆚 How Does Free Compare to Standard?

Here's a detailed comparison to help you choose the right plan:

Feature

Free Plan 🆓

Standard Plan 💼

Cost$0/monthPay-as-you-go pricing
AvailabilityTrial & LiveLive
Number of Schedules15Unlimited
Minimum Interval1 hour ⏰5 minutes ⚡
AuthenticationOAuth 2.0 🔒OAuth 2.0 🔒
Multitenancy✅✅
REST APIFull access ✅Full access ✅
Alert NotificationsUnlimited ✅Unlimited ✅
CF Tasks✅✅
Cloud ALM✅✅
SupportNo, only Community 👥SLA-backed 🛡️
Best ForLearning, small workloads, PoCsProduction, enterprise, high-frequency jobs

💡 Tip: For production workloads requiring SLA-backed support, consider the Standard plan.

When to Choose Free

  • ✅ You need 15 or fewer scheduled jobs
  • ✅ Hourly or less frequent scheduling is sufficient
  • ✅ You're comfortable with community support
  • ✅ You want zero cost scheduling
  • ✅ You're building PoCs, demos, or learning projects

When to Update to Standard

  • ✅ You need more than 15 schedules
  • ✅ You require sub-hourly scheduling (every 5 minutes, etc.)
  • ✅ You need SLA-backed support
  • ✅ You're running business-critical production workloads
  • ✅ You require faster response times from SAP support

💡 Good News: Updating from Free to Standard is seamless! Your jobs, schedules, and configurations are preserved. (continue reading for detailed instructions)

That's it for the overview! Scroll down for a quick reference guide and step-by-step instructions to get started with the Free tier on BTP Live.

🚀 How to create instance of Free Tier on BTP Live

Using the BTP Cockpit

Step 1: Access BTP Cockpit

  1. Log in to your SAP BTP Cockpit
  2. Navigate to your Global Account → Subaccount
  3. Make sure you're in the Live (production) environment

Step 2: Find Job Scheduling Service

  1. In your subaccount, go to Service Marketplace
  2. Search for "Job Scheduling Service"
  3. Click on the service to see available plans

Step 3: Create Free Plan Instance

Using the BTP Cockpit:

  1. Click Create button
  2. Select Service: Job Scheduling Service
  3. Select Plan: free ⭐
  4. Enter an Instance Name: e.g., my-free-scheduler
  5. Optionally configure parameters (usually not needed)
  6. Click Create

Using the Cloud Foundry CLI

# Log in to your CF space
cf login -a <api-endpoint>

# Create free tier instance
cf create-service jobscheduler free my-free-scheduler

# Verify creation
cf service my-free-scheduler

Note: you can check what is available in your region with cf marketplace -e jobscheduler

🔄 Updating from Free to Standard

Need more power? Updating is seamless (see below)! Everything is preserved!

Using the BTP Cockpit

  1. Navigate to your Job Scheduling Service instance in the BTP Cockpit
  2. Click on the instance to view details
  3. Click the Actions dropdown and select Update
  4. Choose the standard plan and press Update Instance
  5. Wait for the update to complete (usually a few seconds)
  6. Restage your app to pick up changes

Using the Cloud Foundry CLI

# Update service plan
cf update-service my-free-scheduler -p standard

# Restage your app to pick up changes

⚠️ Note: Updating to Standard will start incurring charges based on usage. When you update your plan, all executions performed on the same day are charged toward the standard plan.

📚 Additional Resources

Documentation

  • SAP Job Scheduling Service Help
  • Free Plan Service Plans
  • REST API Reference
  • Secure Access Configuration

Discovery Center

  • Job Scheduling Service in Discovery Center

Community

  • All Job Scheduler Blog Posts
  • SAP Community Q&A

Related Blog Posts

  • Christmas Present: Free Plan on BTP Trial
  • Lite Plan Deprecation Notice

🎉 Start Scheduling Today!

The Free tier makes SAP Job Scheduling Service accessible to everyone. Whether you're a student learning the ropes, a developer building a side project, or an enterprise evaluating the service, you can now schedule jobs at zero cost on BTP Live!

⚖️ Terms and Conditions

Free tier service plans are subject to additional terms:

  • ⚠️ No SLA: Service Level Agreements do not apply to free plans
  • ⚠️ Community Support Only: No official SAP support tickets
  • ⚠️ Subject to Terms: Usage governed by Business Technology Platform Supplemental Terms and Conditions
  • ⚠️ Fair Use: SAP reserves the right to enforce fair use policies
  • ⚠️ Service Changes: SAP may modify or discontinue free tier with notice

💡 For production workloads requiring SLAs and official support, update to the Standard plan.

Happy Scheduling! 🎉

The SAP Job Scheduling Service Team

Have questions? Drop a comment below or visit the SAP Community to start a discussion!

entry.title: Re: Has SAP Quietly Removed Secure Login Service from the...
entry.content:
Hi Carsten,

it's "something else". 

The Discovery Center covers "SAP BTP Services". SAP Secure Login Service for SAP GUI is implemented on SAP BTP, but it is part of SAP's portfolio for Governance, Risk and Compliance. That's why we do not offer some of the commercial models that you expect from a SAP BTP service, such as SAP BTPEA.

To avoid confusion on customer side, services that are not part of the BTP portfolio were recently removed from the Discovery Center. Not sure if this reduced or increased the level of confusion 🙂

Best regards,
Christian

entry.title: Validation Messages: CBS and IBS Configuration
entry.content:
As part of the transition to the new CBS (Contribuição sobre Bens e Serviços) and IBS (Imposto sobre Bens e Serviços) tax structure, SAP has introduced three new validation messages when you receive inbound electronic fiscal documents.

Understanding the New Messages 

The messages address common discrepancies that may arise when processing XML documents under Brazil Tax Reform: 

J1B_NFE660: Expected CBS/IBS tax rate is X but tax rate in XML is Y   

This message alerts users when there's a mismatch between the expected tax rate configured in the system and the rate specified in the incoming XML document. 

J1B_NFE661: CBS/IBS tax not available in XML for purchase order item X   

This validation triggers when the required CBS/IBS tax information is missing from the XML for a specific purchase order item. 

J1B_NFE663: Tax group CBS/IBS from XML not available in the current Tax Code   

This message appears when the tax group referenced in the XML document cannot be found in the system's current tax code configuration. 

You can configure each message to appear as either Warning or Error, depending on your business requirements. For detailed instructions on configuring these messages through transaction OBMSG, please refer to Configuring Messages 660, 661, and 663 in OBMSG and OBA5. 

 

Have you found this blog post useful? If so, choose “Like”, share it with your colleagues, and follow me for similar content.  

You are welcome to leave your questions and read similar blog posts on the  SAP Document and Reporting Compliance SAP Community. Don’t forget to follow the  SAP Electronic Invoicing for Brazil (SAP Nota Fiscal Eletronica)  tag and the  SAP Document and Reporting Compliance Community  to keep up with the latest NF-e news.  

Thank you, and until next time,  
Juliana Jarotzky

entry.title: SAP BTP Boosters with Terraform Documentation
entry.content:
Hi DevOps,

since I am tinkering around with Terraform and the SAP BTP Platform. After some ClickOps (Boosters, and otherapproaches) vs. Infrastructure/Configuration As Code (via Terraform and BTP CLI and other REST APIs) experiments later, I miss some better integrated documentation. The "booster" feature within the SAP BTP Cockpit is pretty neat for a fast start and some initial experiments, but pretty intransparent for productive purpose.

Does anyone know, if the various boosters could be exported as a Terraform project (resources, variables and so on)? That would make a review process much easier. Furthermore, some customizing of the configuration would be possible. 

By the way, the Terraform Sample Repo goes already a great direction for documenting some missions. I wish, some other SAP recommended reference architecture would be presented here as well.

Happy coding,

Florian

entry.title: Re: Skipping Inspection lots creation for Inbound delivery
entry.content: The standard behaviour is when you post a goods receipt for an inbound delivery with inspection type 01 (GR for PO) or 05 (GR for inbound delivery), the system creates an inspection lot per batch. That is why in your example, each batch (Batch 1, Batch 2, Batch 3) generates its own inspection lot. To have only one lot per inbound delivery, you need either a special inspection type configuration or a custom enhancement.

entry.title: Receive Events from Event Mesh of Integration Suite to CAP Node.js Applications
entry.content:
In a previous blog, we covered how to publish events from a CAP application to Event Mesh of Integration Suite. This blog post is the natural counterpart. Here, we'll walk through the steps to configure a CAP Node.js application to consume and process events from the Event Mesh of SAP Integration Suite (often referred to as EMIS). Both the CAP application and Event Mesh are in the same BTP subaccount, but it also works if they are provisioned in different BTP subaccounts.

This guide skips the initial Event Mesh provisioning and focuses on the application development, configuration, and deployment steps for event consumption.

Step 1: start by creating a new CAP Node.js application in Business Application Studio

cds init emis-consume
cd emis-consume
touch srv/consume-event.cds
touch srv/consume-event.js

 Step 2: Define the Service Handler in srv/consume-event.cds

service ConsumeEvent @(path: '/consume-event') {
    // Entity definitions are optional for a pure event consumer service
}

Step 3: Define the event handling logic in srv/consume-event.js

const cds = require('@sap/cds');

module.exports = cds.service.impl(async function () {
    const messaging = await cds.connect.to('messaging');

    // Handle events in "$namespace/hello-world" topic
    await messaging.on('hello-world', (msg) => {
        console.log('> hello-world event received: ', msg.data);
    });

    // Handle events in "$namespace/greeting" topic
    await messaging.on('greeting', (msg) => {
        console.log('> greeting event received: ', msg.data);
    });
});

Step 4: add the necessary services for authentication and messaging:

cds add xsuaa --for production
cds add enterprise-messaging-shared --for production

Then update your package.json to configure the messaging service:

"cds": {
  "requires": {
    "production": {
      "auth": "xsuaa",
      "messaging": {
        "kind": "enterprise-messaging-shared",
        "publishPrefix": "$namespace/",
        "subscribePrefix": "$namespace/",
        "format": "cloudevents",
        "vcap": {
          "label": "user-provided",
          "name": "emis-consume-messaging-ups"
        },
        "queue": {
          "name": "$namespace/incoming"
        }
      }
    }
  }
}

Step 5: Prepare the MTA Deployment Descriptor

npm install
cds build --production
cds add mta

You must then edit the generated mta.yaml file to ensure the resource definitions match the configuration in package.json. Specifically, verify that the emis-consume-messaging resource points to the correct user-provided service.

Step 6. Crucial Workaround: Mocking the Management Endpoint

During deployment, cloud foundry runtime attempts to create the queue defined in package.json via a management URI provided by Event Mesh. Currently, this can fail with the error: "Requested route does not match any of the defined routes."

Workaround: 

1). create event mesh service instance & key

  • service name: SAP Integration Suite, Event Mesh
  • service plan: message-client
  • name: emis-consume-messaging

With below parameters:

{
    "namespace": "stc/emis/consume",
    "options": {
        "management": false,
        "messaging": false,
        "messagingrest": false
    },
    "resources": {"units": 10},
    "rules": {
        "queueRules": {
            "publishFilter": ["${namespace}/*"],
            "subscribeFilter": ["${namespace}/*"]
        },
        "topicRules": {
            "publishFilter": ["${namespace}/*"],
            "subscribeFilter": ["${namespace}/*"]
        }
    }
}

Then create a service key and copy its value.

2).  Modify the service-key by replacing its management uri with a mock endpoint. The mockend endpoint should always return 201 when receiving PUT request, for example,

PUT https://stc-mock.free.beeceptor.com/hub/rest/api/v1/management/messaging/queues/testqueue

3). Create a User-Provided Service in your Cloud Foundry space using this modified service key (emis-consume-messaging-ups). This is the service your CAP application will bind to.

 

4). Login to Event Mesh of Integration Suite >> Configure, create the queue stc/emis/consume/incoming

5). add below topic subscription to the queue

  • stc/emis/consume/hello-world
  • stc/emis/consume/greeting

This configuration ensures any event published to these topics is delivered to your application's queue.

Step 7: Build and Deploy

mbt build
cf deploy mta_archives/emis-consume_1.0.0.mtar

Step 8: Testing

Go to the Test section of your Event Mesh in the Integration Suite. Send test events to the topics hello-world and greeting. 

In the BTP cloud foundry cockpit, navigate to your deployed application and check its logs.

You have successfully configured a CAP Node.js application to act as an event consumer from Event Mesh of SAP Integration Suite. Although we had to implement a workaround due to a current compatibility issue between CAP's queue management and Event Mesh's Management API, the overall pattern remains sound and functional. Hopefully, SAP will address this compatibility issue soon, making the developer journey even smoother.

entry.title: Temporary Login Delay after ABAP Trial 2602 Upgrade
entry.content:
Dear SAP Community,

We want to inform you about a known issue affecting new ABAP Trial service instances following this weekend’s 2602 upgrade.

What’s happening:
Users who provision a new ABAP Trial instance from the SAP BTP Marketplace after the upgrade may experience a temporary delay before they can successfully log in for the first time.

Who is affected:
This issue only impacts newly created ABAP Trial instances. If you already have an existing ABAP Trial service instance, you are not affected and can continue working as usual.

Expected behavior:
Initial login access may take up to one hour after instance creation completes. Subsequent logins will not be affected.

Recommended action:
If you’re creating a new ABAP Trial instance this weekend or next week, please allow up to 60 minutes after provisioning before attempting your first login. We apologize for any inconvenience this may cause.

Resolution timeline:
Our team is actively working on a resolution, which is expected to be deployed by Tuesday, February 17th. We’ll update this thread once the issue is fully addressed.

Thank you for your patience and understanding.

Best regards,
Paul

entry.title: Error in Year Closing due to AUCs with Net Book Value Negative in Depreciation Areas
entry.content:
Hi FI Experts,

We are facing an issue while trying to close the Fixed Asset Year 2024. The system is displaying an error for two AUCs stating: “Net Book Value is negative in area 01 & 15.”
Our configuration is set to “Only Positive Values or Zero Allowed.”

Although both AUCs show positive or zero values in AW01N, the system still triggers the negative NBV error during year-end closing. We have thoroughly reviewed both AUCs but could not identify the cause.  Expert guidance is urgently required to resolve this issue. Relevant screenshots are attached for your kind review.

entry.title: Migrating from ASANWEE to ASAAEMEE: From SAP Event Mesh to SAP Advanced Event Mesh
entry.content:
Introduction

Event-driven architectures are increasingly shaping modern SAP landscapes. Many customers started their journey using the NetWeaver add-on for event enablement (component ASANWEE) to publish business events from their SAP systems to SAP Event Mesh.

While this setup works well for basic SAP BTP scenarios, growing enterprise requirements — such as hybrid integration, advanced routing, and high availability — often require a more powerful event broker.

This is where the Event Add-On for ERP (ASAAEMEE) in combination with SAP Advanced Event Mesh becomes highly relevant.

This article applies to:

  • SAP ERP (ECC 6.0 and higher)

  • SAP S/4HANA (on-premise, private cloud, all versions)

and explains the technical migration steps from component ASANWEE to ASAAEMEE.

 

Prerequisits:

  • NetWeaver Add-On for Event Enablement is installed (component ASANWEE).
  • Event Add-on for ERP is installed (component ASAAEMEE, installed on top of ASANWEE).
  • SAP Advanced Event Mesh service instance is provisioned.
  • Service Key from the BTP is available.
  • Ensure that the erp_event_add_on entitlement is added to your subaccount in the SAP BTP cockpit.
  • The SAP Integration Suite, Advanced Event Mesh instance must be of type standard plan, not default plan. For details on migrating to the standard service plan, refer to the SAP Advanced Event Mesh migration guide.

 

Migration Steps

Step 1: Create RFC-Destinations.

To establish the connection to the SAP Advanced Event Mesh, we will create three RFC-Destinations in transaction SM59.

1. Create RFC-Destination pointing to the REST endpoint of the Advanced Event Mesh (Available inside the AEM broker).

  • Type: G (HTTP Connection to External Server).

  • Target Host: AEM REST endpoint host.
  • Basic Authentication: Add credentials, listed in the AEM broker for the REST endpoint.
  • SSL: Active.
  • Connection test should return in a 405 Method Not Allowed.

      

   

2. Create RFC-Destination for the Handshake (Available inside the SAP BTP Service Key).

  • Type: G

  • Target Host: Handshake URL from the SAP BTP Service Key
  • Path Prefix: /handshake
  • SSL: Active.

3. Create RFC-Destination for the Token (Available inside the SAP BTP Service Key).

  • Type: G

  • Target Host: Token endpoint from Service Key
  • Path Prefix: /oauth/token
  • SSL: Active.

   

 

Step 2: Add Certificates to the Trust Store.

To enable secure communication with SAP Advanced Event Mesh, the server certificate must be trusted in your SAP system.

  • Open transaction STRUST.
  • Select the SSL Client PSE that is used in the RFC destination created in SM59 (usually SSL Client (Anonymous) unless a specific PSE was assigned).
  • Click Import Certificate.
  • Upload the certificate file (.cer / .crt).
  • Click Add to Certificate List and save.

 

Step 3: Enable the Event Add-On for ERP.

Inside your SAP Advanced Event Mesh, navigate to Event Add-On for ERP.

After enabling the service, the component can be installed on your SAP system.

 

 

Step 4: Activate BC-Set.

Business Configuration Sets contain customizing and configuration-related table entries required for proper communication with SAP Advanced Event Mesh.

Transaction: SCPR20

  • Enter BC-Set name:/ASADEV/ACI_BCSET_FRAMEWORK_AEM
  • Choose Activate.

 

Step 5: Copy Instance for SAP Event Mesh to new Instance for SAP Advanced Event Mesh.

  • Go to SPRO -> IMG -> Integration with Other SAP Components -> SAP NetWeaver AddOn for Event enablement -> Connection and Replication Object Customizing 
  • Or go directly to transaction /ASADEV/ACI_SETTINGS.
  • Copy the existing SAP Event Mesh instance to create a new SAP Advanced Event Mesh instance:

  • Replace RFC destinations with the ones created for AEM
  • Change Cloud Type from SAP_EM to SAP_AEM.

  • Hit Enter and copy all.
  • Skip errors for duplicate Event Linkages

  • Delete all Default Values (not required for SAP AEM):

  • Delete Error Type Mapping (not required for SAP AEM):

  • You can keep the message type in case you delete the old Instance pointing to the SAP Event Mesh.
  • Update message types if keeping the old SAP_EM instance::
    • Create new Message Type in transaction WE81.
    • Activate Change Pointer for the new message type in transaction BD50.

  • Add Event Linkages for the new message type:

 

  • Go to Header Attributes.
    • Delete all Header Attributes for SAP EM.
    • Add new Header Attributes for SAP AEM.

 

  • Go to Field Mapping and configure a dynamic topic.
    • Each level becomes a segment in the dynamic topic.
    • There are static segments (Default Values) and dynamic segments (Source structure and Source field).

 

Step 6: Add validation Instance for the SAP BTP.

  • Got to Connections and add New Entry.
  • A Validation Instance will be necessary for the authentication against the BTP.
  • Add RFC destination for Handshake (from Step 1).

 

  • Go to Default Values and specify:
    • ACI_CLIENT_ID: Client ID from the Service Key.
    • ACI_TOKEN_DESTINATION: RFC-Destination for the Token (from Step 1).
    • AUTH_TYPE: OAUTH (Certificate also possible; refer to official documentation).

  • Add client secret from the Service Key
    • Transaction: SPRO
    • Go to: Integration with Other SAP Components -> SAP NetWeaver AddOn for Event enablement -> Set the cloud connection password
    • Or go directly to transaction: /ASADEV/SCI_TPW
    • Select the Cloud Instance for validation: SAP_VALIDATION
    • Enter the client secret in the Cloud Shared Secret field and execute.

 

Step 7: Test the new connection to SAP Advanced Event Mesh.

After the steps from above we can now test our new connection to the SAP Advanced Event Mesh.

Change something in the Application, where your Event Add-on for ERP does have an Oubtound Object configured and check the monitor transaction /ASADEV/ACI_MONITOR.

Cloud response should be 200 OK.

On the left side of the Trace we can see the JSON and dynamic topic, that was used to send the message to the SAP Advances Event Mesh.

This was configured inside the Field Mapping of the Outbound Object.

Migration Completed

After these steps, your SAP system is successfully switched from SAP Event Mesh to SAP Advanced Event Mesh using ASAAEMEE.

  • No changes are required in event definitions.

  • Monitoring and validation ensure messages are delivered correctly.
  • Find more configuration options for component ASAAEMEE inside the SAP Discovery Center mission "Data Exchange to Connect SAP ERP with SAP Advanced Event Mesh for Event-Driven Integration".

entry.title: Custom CDS Views – What’s New up to 2602
entry.content:
Introduction and Overview

Over the last releases 2502 to 2602, we’ve delivered a number of improvements to the Custom CDS Views app in SAP S/4HANA. In this post, I’d like to share the most relevant customer-facing changes, along with a few behind-the-scenes updates that help improve usability, performance, and overall reliability.
This update is especially relevant for key users working with extensibility and analytics in SAP S/4HANA:

  • UX Improvements
  • Better Handling of Switched Fields
  • Expert Settings
  • Behind the Scenes
  • What’s Next

UX Improvements

With release 2602, the Custom CDS Views app received a refreshed layout aligned with the latest SAP Fiori design guidelines. The main goal was to further harmonize the experience with other extensibility and analytics apps and make daily work a bit more intuitive.
Some highlights you may notice:
  • A new General tab that now contains attributes previously shown in the header
  • A leaner header with improved information density
  • Updated table styling for a more consistent look and feel across the key user apps
In addition, buttons that do not finalize an edit have been moved from the footer to the header. As a result, the footer bar is now only shown in edit mode, where it is actually needed.

The new "General" tab

Better Handling of Switched Fields (as of 2508 and 2025)

Working with switches can sometimes be tricky. To make this more transparent, the app now clearly indicates when elements depend on a switch.
  • Fields that are not available in your system due to the current switch state no longer appear in value helps. This helps avoid adding fields by mistake
  • Elements that are already part of a Custom CDS View but are affected by switches are highlighted accordingly

This should make it easier to understand why certain elements are (or are not) available in your system.

A switched field in the "Add Elements" dialogue  A toggled field on the "Elements" tab

Expert Settings (as of 2602)

With 2602, we introduced Expert Settings in the General tab. You can reveal them using Show more. As the name suggests, these settings are intended for advanced scenarios and should only be adjusted if you know what you are doing or have been advised by an expert or SAP Support.
Currently, the only available expert setting controls whether the SAP HANA execution engine should use HEX for query processing. Depending on the scenario, HEX can improve performance or influence how a query is executed.

The "Expert Settings" on the "General" tab

If you want to dive deeper, take a look at:
  • ABAP Data Modeling Guide (performance section)
  • ABAP CDS Language Documentation for annotation @Consumption.dbHints

Behind the Scenes

In addition to visible UI and feature changes, we also continued to improve the technical foundation of the app.
The changes we worked on may not be noticed immediately, but they help ensure a smoother overall experience:
  • Parts of the old Custom CDS Views app were still used internally for the raw data preview. This preview has now been fully reimplemented and integrated into the new app.
  • We once again optimized the calculation of rework tasks, making it more reliable and less resource-intensive.

What's Next

We recently completed another Customer Engagement Initiative, which provided valuable feedback and lots of good ideas. We heard you and are now evaluating some of the focus topics of the initiative:
  • Supporting a more confident selection of data sources
  • Improving the usability of the code editor for calculated fields and filters
  • Providing better insights into performance and complexity
  • Making errors and warnings easier to understand and act upon
We’re also taking another look at the calculation of rework tasks. While rework task availability is already visible in production systems, upcoming improvements aim to make this calculation more flexible and more transparent.
As always, feedback from the community is highly appreciated, so feel free to share your thoughts and experiences in the comments.

entry.title: Re: Behavior of Advanced Foreign Currency Valuation after Clearing
entry.content:
Further regarding the topic of foreign exchange variation, we have the following scenario:

At the moment of payment, we need to separate what is realized foreign exchange variation from what is foreign exchange gain/loss.

Example:
When the vendor invoice is in BRL, the exchange difference account should be 723010002 – Gain/Loss – Cash and Equivalents, meaning gain/loss variation.

When the vendor invoice is in USD, the exchange difference account should be 712020001 – Real FX Loss or 712020002 – Real FX Gain, meaning realized variation.

Is it possible to perform this segregation?

    1. Valuation posted to a gain/loss account when the vendor invoice is issued in the company code currency.

    2. Valuation posted to a realized exchange rate difference account when the vendor invoice is issued in a foreign currency.

    Is it possible to handle both scenarios through standard configuration? If so, could you please provide the detailed configuration steps required?

entry.title: Re: Trigger Custom API on Create & Save of Warehouse Task in /SCWM/POST (EWM)
entry.content:
Hello @Saoud 

First, the /SCWM/EX_CORE_CO_POST BAdI is executed when posting a confirmed warehouse task:
Most probably that's why it doesn't work for you.

Second, if you insist on BAdI-based solution, you should use /SCWM/EX_CORE_CR_POST.

Third, I would strongly recommend a custom PPF action triggered after warehouse order creation, that would call your API. See SAP Help: Integration of PPF and the Condition Technique That is much better than a raw BAdI-based approach. PPF gives you visibility of what and when was triggered, as well as error handling, reprocessing and logging.

Best regards

Dominik Tylczynski

entry.title: Re: SAP Public Cloud 2602 / MDG - How to Mass Import Data...
entry.content:
Dear @LukasKuhn,

Case 1.

Based on your Validation Log messages Action I is not allowed for E-Mails table ADR6 and for table ADRCT this is not a user error in the Excel formatting it is the system telling you that INSERT is not supported for those tables in this mass process/template. In Public Cloud documentation for file upload/mass processing, SAP explicitly differentiates which tables support adding/deleting rows for example ADR6 is explicitly listed in the context of delete-support which aligns with what you see delete is supported but INSERT not

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/f86dc2eb1f8b48c880a7607213104b27/0b79741524714f8982819964874a35d7.html

What to do instead:

  • Maintain the first e-mail / remark in the Maintain Business Partner / Manage Business Partner Master Data app manual but standard

Case 2.

This typically happens for one of these reasons in Public Cloud mass maintenance:

1. You exported too narrow and missed mandatory/key fields for the text record

  • For KNVV_STXL, the validation requires key fields such as LANGU and COUNTRY to be provided in the upload file exactly as expected by the template (same columns/format as in the system export), otherwise the long text entry cannot be validated.
  • Best practice is re-export the same BP again but don’t manually trim the file down to only a couple of columns/fields. Keep all underlined key fields from the exported sheet and only change the text content fields

2. You tried to use _ACTION_CODE when you actually only want to UPDATE

  • For updating existing entries, SAP own Public Cloud guidance says you generally don’t need an action code _ACTION_CODE = I is for inserts and can trigger different validation expectations

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3506176

  • Also SAP file-upload documentation describes _ACTION_CODE usage and the standard values (Insert/Copy/Delete)

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/f86dc2eb1f8b48c880a7607213104b27/0b79741524714f8982819964874a35d7.html

Troubleshooting check steps:

  1. Re-export the BP for this exact sales area including KNVV_STXL and don’t remove key columns
  2. In the KNVV_STXL sheet, unhide columns and confirm LANGU and COUNTRY or the country key field name used in your template are filled exactly as in the export
  3. Change only the text value fields you intend to change and leave _ACTION_CODE empty for an update

If the error still persists even with a fresh template export check whether the BP master data itself is missing mandatory values such as Country/Region or Language Key for the standard address

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3248014

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Sales Kits processing using third-party item categories
entry.content:
Options if you need PRs for kits

  1. Alternative design: Instead of using 31Q, model the kit as a standard material with item category set to third‑party. This way, the entire kit triggers a PR.
  2. Custom extension: In Public Cloud, you cannot change item category determination for kit components. If the business requirement is critical, you would need to explore extensibility (e.g., custom logic in item category determination), but this is limited in Public Cloud.
  3. Process workaround: Treat the kit as a non‑stock material and use the third‑party sales process (BDK) so that a PR is generated for the vendor to supply the kit.

entry.title: SE78: “Logo not found” error when sending mail with inline image
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I am facing an issue while sending emails that include an inline logo image.

During the email sending process, the system throws a “Logo not found” error related to SE78, and the email is not sent.

What I Have Checked

  • Verified that the logo exists in SE78

  • Confirmed that the logo name is correctly referenced in the program

  • Checked SCOT configuration

  • SMTP is working correctly

  • Executed RSCONN01 manually

Despite these checks, the error persists.

Question

Are there additional settings or dependencies in SE78 that could cause this issue?

Is there anything specific I should verify regarding logo storage type (BMAP/BDS) or client dependency?

 

The code block is below.

REPORT zsg_html_mail_with_logo.

DATA: lo_bcs       TYPE REF TO cl_bcs,
      lo_document  TYPE REF TO cl_document_bcs,
      lo_recipient TYPE REF TO if_recipient_bcs.

DATA: lt_html TYPE soli_tab.

DATA: lt_bds_content TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF bapiconten,
      lt_content_hex TYPE solix_tab,
      lv_xstring     TYPE xstring,
      lv_size        TYPE so_obj_len,
      lv_input_len   TYPE i.

CALL FUNCTION 'SAPSCRIPT_GET_GRAPHIC_BDS'
  EXPORTING
    i_object  = 'GRAPHICS'
    i_name    = 'HTML_MAIL_LOGO'     
    i_id      = 'BMAP'
    i_btype   = 'BCOL'
  TABLES
    content   = lt_bds_content
  EXCEPTIONS
    not_found = 1
    OTHERS    = 2.

IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  MESSAGE 'Logo not found in SE78' TYPE 'E'.
ENDIF.

lv_input_len = lines( lt_bds_content ) * 255.

CALL FUNCTION 'SCMS_BINARY_TO_XSTRING'
  EXPORTING
    input_length = lv_input_len
  IMPORTING
    buffer       = lv_xstring
  TABLES
    binary_tab   = lt_bds_content
  EXCEPTIONS
    failed       = 1
    OTHERS       = 2.

IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  MESSAGE 'Binary to XSTRING conversion failed' TYPE 'E'.
ENDIF.

CALL FUNCTION 'SCMS_XSTRING_TO_BINARY'
  EXPORTING
    buffer     = lv_xstring
  TABLES
    binary_tab = lt_content_hex.

lv_size = xstrlen( lv_xstring ).

APPEND '<html><body>' TO lt_html.
APPEND '<h2>HTML Mail Test</h2>' TO lt_html.
APPEND '<img src="cid:logo_cid">' TO lt_html.
APPEND '<p>This email was sent via SAP ABAP using CL_BCS.</p>' TO lt_html.
APPEND '</body></html>' TO lt_html.

lo_bcs = cl_bcs=>create_persistent( ).

lo_document = cl_document_bcs=>create_document(
  i_type    = 'HTM'
  i_text    = lt_html
  i_subject = 'Inline Image HTML Mail Test' ).

lo_bcs->set_document( lo_document ).

lo_document->add_attachment(
  i_attachment_type    = 'PNG'
  i_attachment_subject = 'Logo'
  i_attachment_size    = lv_size
  i_att_content_hex    = lt_content_hex
  i_attachment_header  = VALUE soli_tab(
                           ( line = 'Content-ID: <logo_cid>' )
                           ( line = 'Content-Disposition: inline' ) ) ).

lo_recipient =
  cl_cam_address_bcs=>create_internet_address( 'xxxx.yyyy@gmail.com' ).

lo_bcs->add_recipient( lo_recipient ).

lo_bcs->send( ).

COMMIT WORK.

WRITE: 'Mail successfully sent.'.

entry.title: Re: Employee Time Off Request auto approval before Payrol...
entry.content:
Hi @rsriramm ,

This is quite an interesting requirement, and although I wouldn’t call myself a CPI expert, there is a logical flow you can follow to build a robust and automated solution. Think of it as a three‑step journey: first identifying the right workflow requests, then changing the approver, and finally approving them through the API.

Step 1 — Identifying the Workflow Requests

Our journey begins by figuring out which workflow requests actually meet your criteria.
To do this, I would create the initial integration flow to retrieve all employee time records where the end date is on or before the payroll cut‑off date and pending for approval.

You can control this dynamically by scheduling the job to run every 14 days and filtering the records using today’s date (end date on or before today). Since each employee time record includes the field workflowRequestId, this becomes the key piece of data we need. Once identified, store those workflow request IDs in a data store so they can be processed in the next steps.

Step 2 — Reassigning the Approver to the API User

Before the system can approve any workflow automatically, the approval rights need to be aligned. Since a workflow can only be approved by its designated approver, the next step is to change the approver to your API user.

Using the workflow request IDs stored earlier, you can trigger changeWfRequestApprover to update the approver accordingly.

Step 3 — Approving the Workflow Requests

Now that the API user is officially the approver, the final step becomes straightforward.
Using the same dataset from your data store, call the approveWfRequest to complete the workflow approvals automatically.

 
I hope this gives you a solid structure to build your solution.

Regards,
VR

entry.title: Re: check is not getting assigned in public cloud
entry.content:
Dear @muhammad_yousufali,

If neither Manage Automatic Payments (F0770) nor Create Single Payment (F0743) assigns a check number, then in Public Cloud it typically means the system cannot determine a valid checkbook/check lot for payment method C most commonly due to an inconsistent / multiple-chain checkbook setup or an exhausted check lot. Output Parameter Determination only affects printing after the check exists it does not create the check number

SAP Notes below for this situation that I sent in my first answer:
• https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2706870
• https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3605460
• https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3352922

The quickest way to confirm which case you hit is the exact error text in the payment run log / message because it usually matches one of the above SAP Notes directly

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: SAP Automation Pilot – Empowering IT Operations with AI Insights
entry.content:
I am very happy to share this interesting scenario to you inspired by the powerful collaboration between Johnson Controls International (JCI), the SAP MaxAttention, and the SAP Automation Pilot team, showcasing how automation and AI are reshaping enterprise IT operations. Kudos to everyone from the teams involved! 

Note: a link to the public Customer Story here: JCI: Building a global foundation for smart growth by strengthening IT reliability and efficiency

The Challenge: Monitoring Complexity at Scale

JCI operates a large and complex SAP ERP landscape — spanning more than 30 SAP Enterprise Management systems. For an enterprise company — system uptime and reliability are non-negotiable.

Until recently, JCI relied on manual monitoring, with IT staff logging in every six hours to track KPIs, collect system data, and prepare reports. This process was time-consuming, repetitive, and resource-intensive, often resulting in outdated data and increased risk of unplanned downtime.

The Solution: AI-Powered Automation with SAP Automation Pilot

To tackle this challenge, JCI turned to SAP Automation Pilot integrated with SAP AI Core service. The team implemented an innovative solution that automates the collection of system health KPIs and generates intelligent insights using generative AI — all within a low-code environment.

This setup replaced thousands of hours of manual work with an automated, intelligent monitoring process across JCI’s SAP landscape. The automation not only removed the risk of missed issues but also ensured 24/7 visibility into system performance and health.

Working closely with the SAP MaxAttention and SAP Automation Pilot team,  JCI gained the benefit of guidance, innovation discovery workshops, hands-on architectural and implementation support, helping transform vision into a fully functional, scalable pilot.

“SAP Automation Pilot enables automated collection of system health KPIs and generates insights using generative AI in a low-code environment,” shared Chandra Talasila from JCI team. 

See below an example of a reference automation flow modelled and run in SAP Automation Pilot to automated systems' health status  AI-powered insights. 

The Business Impact: Continuity and Efficiency

The results were immediate and tangible, see below:

  • 3,000+ hours saved annually through automation
  • Significant cost savings and productivity gains
  • Elimination of unplanned downtime risk, ensuring continuity across manufacturing and operations

This shift has freed up IT teams to focus on high-value work, transforming system monitoring from a reactive task into a strategic capability that supports proactive decision-making and operational resilience.

Looking Ahead: Expanding AI and Automation

Building on this successful foundation, JCI is now accelerating its AI and automation roadmap. Future plans include:

  • Automating pre- and post-maintenance validations
  • Streamlining recurring IT tickets
  • Deploying digital tools for outage communication management
  • Exploring SAP’s Business AI offerings, including SAP Joule and SAP EarlyWatch Alert, for broader integration and insight delivery

Looking ahead, SAP MaxAttention and SAP Automation Pilot will continue to play a key role in supporting these forward-looking plans—empowering SAP customers to embrace AI, accelerate innovation, and bring tangible value in their day-to-day operations. As AI becomes an integral part of enterprise IT strategy, our commitment remains strong: to partner with customers at every step, enabling smarter and more resilient business outcomes.

In case you have any comments and / or ideas for other automation scenarios - please do not hesitate to leave a comment or reach out directly at biser.simeonov@sap.com

entry.title: Re: Switch Framework-SAP
entry.content:
@Shruthisampath Thx for your blog post. This led me to the "newly" available switch type "L"

SFW1 screenshot with "Lightweight switch" 

 

Here the SAP Help Link for "Switching Client-Specific Lightweight Business Functions"
https://help.sap.com/docs/ABAP_PLATFORM_NEW/1d51ac4c098042528cadb1d5bcb1ab23/2f3adaba4e364e33b90c0582be1d5d89.html?locale=en-US&version=LATEST

Authorization object is "S_SWITCH_C". Transport object SFSW.

Make sure you have the newest available SNOTE for component "BC-DWB-TOO-SFW" applied to your system:
3558193 Consistency check of switched CDS view extensions without findings
3566530 Logging Improvement for SFW Activation in Technical Configuration
3603574 Fix a possibility for state inconsistency after client copy
3633396 SFW: Skip prerequisite check for switched-on business functions
3690778 Exclude Feature Toggle check during DDIC Import

entry.title: Re: Automating Invoice Predictions from SAP Databricks with SAP Datasphere Task Chain
entry.content:
hi@Sebastian_Gesiarz 

BDC Connect enables a secure, bidirectional zero-copy sharing of data products by preserving valuable semantics and business context between SAP BDC and partner systems like Databricks, Google, Microsoft or Snowflake.

SAP Databricks is using a similar technology out of the box of BDC.

 

If you absolutely want to pull the data from your S3 bucket, you could eventually use RepFlow to replicate from the S3 bucket into the DSP HDLFS, but this will then be a completely different scenario...

entry.title: AI’s Role in Organizational Change Management
entry.content:
Let's be honest: most change managers spend more time maintaining spreadsheets than having the conversations that matter or leveraging their empathy and personal skills. AI won't replace OCM—but it can handle the pattern-heavy work, freeing you for strategy, coaching, and navigating organizational dynamics. This post explores how AI augments the six core OCM dimensions in SAP transformations, based on our real project experience.

As part of the OCM Digital Change and Adoption team within SAP’s Consulting Services organization, we lead SAP Organizational Change Management in various  SAP transformations. We recognize that Artificial Intelligence (AI) is reshaping also our work —introducing new opportunities and challenges across how we plan, lead, and sustain change. This post explores how AI could influence our Organizational Change Management (OCM) approach in SAP transformations and beyond.

Importantly, these are practitioner perspectives—not product announcements. Our intent is to share how we see the six OCM dimensions evolving, based on our project experience. To keep the focus on opportunities, we deliberately set aside topics such as GDPR[1] and AI ethics here—though they remain essential considerations in practice.

As OCM practitioners, we advise our customers throughout their transformation journeys. Our approach is aligned to the SAP Activate methodology and addresses six core dimensions, underpinned by data-driven solutions.

Below, you’ll find each dimension, how we apply it today, and how AI could augment it. For clarity, we’ve included a graphic of the six OCM dimensions here.

 

Impact key:

  • 🟩 Medium: Broader acceleration and decision support
  • 🟦 Medium to High: Significant scaling and personalization potential
  • 🟪 High: Step-change in effectiveness and proactivity

 

🟦 Change Strategy sets direction, aligns stakeholders, and prioritizes the people side of the transformation.
Today: We tailor the change approach to your program context, define scope and outcomes, map stakeholders, and plan governance, milestones, and enablement strategies using SAP good practices. 
With AI: We auto‑draft change strategies, stakeholder snapshots, and governance calendars from your business case, project charter, and discovery notes; synthesize interview transcripts into themes and risks; and run scenario comparisons to test plan options. AI also generates first‑cut decks and exec summaries that consultants refine with context.
Impact: Faster strategy and planning cycles, clearer stakeholder alignment early on, and fewer mid‑stream rework loops.

🟩 Change Leadership helps sponsors and managers lead with clarity, consistency, and credibility.

Today: We align leadership on vision and roles, map stakeholders, and equip sponsors with engagement plans, talking points, and routines that sustain momentum.
With AI: We analyze stakeholder networks and sentiment to identify influencers and resistance patterns, recommend an optimal change network for your context, and generate sponsor briefings tailored by audience and region. AI also suggests targeted outreach sequences and tracks follow‑through.
Impact: Faster stakeholder mapping, higher sponsor visibility and touchpoints, earlier detection of at‑risk groups. Which can improve confidence and speed of leadership action.

🟪 Change Communication helps you keep stakeholders informed, engaged, and aligned throughout the transformation.

Today: We craft communication plans, key messages, and stakeholder-specific content based on persona analysis and project milestones.
With AI: We can generate personalized newsletters, FAQs, and leadership talking points in multiple languages and formats, whatever fits the project. E.e. leverage transcripts of AMA Sessions to create a blog in your sharepoint incl a Q&A list.  AI analyzes and summarizes sentiment from your employee feedback channels to adjust messaging before resistance escalates.
Impact: Faster and more personalized content creation, higher engagement rates, real-time neutral sentiment tracking.

🟩 Change Realization turns strategy into targeted actions that move people from current to future ways of working.

Today: We assess process, role, and location impacts; prioritize change measures; and coordinate workshops to translate findings into concrete action plans.
With AI: We accelerate impact analysis from design assets and process maps, cluster similar impacts across your units, and auto‑draft tailored action plans and playbooks fitting for your project—reducing reliance on lengthy manual workshops while preserving expert review.
Impact: Faster impact‑to‑action cycle, fewer workshop hours, clearer prioritization across roles and regions. So that the projects change activities translate into visible progress sooner.

🟦 Change Enablement equips teams and end users with the skills and confidence to adopt new solutions and processes.

Today: We design learning strategies, build curricula, and deliver role‑based training, job aids, and hypercare support aligned to milestones.
With AI: We generate role‑specific learning paths, adapt content to proficiency and language, create just‑in‑time guidance, and use usage data to recommend next‑best learning, tailored to your needs, for this you can use SAP’s Learning Needs Analysis (LNA) microapp. AI generates drafts automatic from sources such as Signavio, SAP Best Practices or CALM.  In the flow of work, you can combine this with WalkMe Learning Arc (where available) to deliver in‑app, role‑aware walkthroughs and micro‑learning that reinforce new behaviors at the point of need. AI also drafts quizzes, simulations, demos, exercises and scenario‑based practice from source materials and managed via Prompts.
Impact: Faster content creation, easier content updates, higher course completion and satisfaction, reduced time‑to‑competence post go‑live. This can improve the adoption where it matters most in the project.

🟪 Change Effectiveness ensures change activities deliver outcomes and remain sustainable over time.

Today: We define KPIs, collect feedback, run health checks, and apply corrective actions based on adoption, performance, and sentiment trends.
With AI: We combine digital adoption checks, benchmarks, and pattern detection to spot emerging risks, forecast slippage, and recommend targeted interventions before issues escalate. AI evaluates and summarizes your feedback channels and explains variance across different sites or roles in your company.
Impact: Risks identified several weeks earlier before they impact your project value, fewer late‑stage escalations, continuous, fact based adoption insights with numbers.

With these six dimensions, you can transition from the status quo to future-ready ways of working more smoothly —so you can realize the full value of your SAP solutions. AI doesn’t replace the human side of change; it amplifies it. It can free consultants and customers to focus on higher-value activities: judgement, alignment, personalization and leadership.

This post reflects our experience and practitioner view of where AI can deliver value today.Agentic AI might even bring more oppportunities.While we intentionally focus on possibilities here, topics such as GDPR, security, and responsible AI are non‑negotiable in delivery and must be addressed in any implementation.

In our next blog post, we’ll dive deeper into the AI × OCM opportunities from a change manager’s perspective, sharing concrete use cases and practical recommendations.

Let us know what you think—we’d love to hear your perspective and experiences.

[1] GDPR: General Data Protection Regulation (DSGVO in German)

entry.title: Expanding Visual Analysis: New Area Chart, Pareto Chart, and Stacked Area Enhancements
entry.content:
As analytics adoption grows, Story Designers are increasingly expected to move beyond static reporting and deliver visual analysis that explains trends, highlights drivers, and supports prioritization.

While SAP Analytics Cloud already offers a strong foundation of visualizations, designers often faced limitations when trying to identify key contributors behind a metric, show how values evolve over time without visual clutter, or clearly communicate proportional impact and cumulative effects.

With the 2026 Q1 QRC, SAP Analytics Cloud expands its out-of-the-box visualization capabilities with new and enhanced charts: Pareto Chart, Area Chart, and improvements to the Stacked Area!

Pareto Chart: Focus on What Truly Drives Impact

The Pareto Chart is designed to help Story Designers to prioritize focus, explain trade-offs, and guide action instead of treating all contributors as equally important.

We wanted the creation of a Pareto Chart to be intentionally simple. Story Designers just have to bind a primary structure (Measure or Account depending on the Model Type) and select the dimension across which the analysis should run. The Pareto Visualization is then automatically generated, sorted from highest to lowest, including the cumulative percentage which increases to 100%. 

Furthermore, we allow additional Primary Structures as supplementary columns for context without impacting the Cumulative Percentage Calculation. It provides Story Designers the option to enrich the analysis while keeping the pareto logic clean and mathematically correct.

⚠️ For the Cumulative Percentage, Pareto Charts rely on a Running Total Calculation and therefore is not supported for data models that do not allow for the creation of a Running Total Calculation in Stories, such as SAP BW and SAP IBP. 

Area Chart: A Clear Alternative to Stacked Visuals

The new Area Chart introduces a powerful option for visualizing trends over time while maintaining focus on the overall shape and direction of the data. Compared to Stacked Area Charts, Area Charts emphasize trend and magnitude rather than composition, reduce visual complexity when multiple series are present, and make it easier to follow a single measure’s evolution across time. It makes Area Charts especially valuable when the analytical goal is trend interpretation rather than part-to-whole comparison.

Stacked Area Chart Enhancements: More Expressive, More Flexible

The Stacked Area Chart has been enhanced to support use cases that matter to you the most! These include the introduction of a 100% Stacked Area Chart, enabling designers to clearly visualize proportional contribution over time. We've also added the configuration to adjust the line to a smooth line, which also applies to the Area Chart and offers consistency with our other Chart Types. 

Why This Matters for Story Designers

These visualization enhancements significantly expand what Story Designers can do out of the box in SAP Analytics Cloud rather than relying on Custom Widgets.

As SAP Analytics Cloud continues to evolve its visualization portfolio, designers can rely less on workarounds and more on purpose-built visuals that match analytical intent.

ⓘ Keep an eye out on the roadmap explorer, as new Chart Types such as the Gauge, Funnel, and Sankey will be coming soon!

entry.title: Are you ready to share your HANA insights with the community?
entry.content:
Are you ready to share your HANA insights with our community?

HANA Tech Con will take place on July 16, and we’re looking for experts, users, and partners to delight us with engaging and inspiring sessions.

Ready to submit your proposal? Here is how:

1: Go to the HANA Tech Con website and click “Call for Proposals” on the main menu:

 

You can also find this option directly on the page by clicking on the button “Become a Speaker”:

2: Once you are inside the Call for Proposals page, you will find detailed information about the topics we are looking for.

💡Tip: We especially appreciate original, relevant, and engaging content.

 

3: Scroll to the end of the page and click the “Submit Your Talk” button:

 

4: Once you reach the submission page, you’ll need to choose a title for your Session:                 

 

Here are some examples from the first HANA Tech Con edition:

- SAP HANA HotSpots

- Mastering HANA Performance

- Database Analysis Using the SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph Engine

- How to optimize the SAP HANA Memory Sizing Predictions

 

5: Select your Session Type based on:

  • TALK (Spot Talk or Deep Dive Talk);
  • PPTless Demo;
  • OTHER (If no format that aligns with your vision, just don’t forget to use the remarks field, not description, to describe your proposed session format). 
  • Extensive Talk.

The REMARKS FIELD is located just below the Description section:

 

6: Tell us about yourself!

If you’re presenting the session alone, please fill in your Name, E-mail and Company.

If your session includes another speaker, please click “Add Speaker” and complete their information as well. You can add up to eight speakers per session:

 

7: To submit your proposal, check the Consent Checkbox and click Submit Proposal.

 

MAKE SURE YOU HAVE SUBMITED YOUR PROPOSAL:

8: After submitting, you’ll see a confirmation page:

 

IMPORTANT INFORMATION:

  • Do NOT FORGET to verify your email, otherwise your proposal won’t be processed. You have 7 days after submitting your proposal to verify it.
  • Once verified, you will receive a link to view or edit your proposal.
  • You MAY submit more than one proposal.
  • Call for speakers closes at 11:59 pm on March 13, 2025.
  • Results will be communicated by email within 2–3 weeks after the deadline.

In case you have any questions, we’re here for you!

Contact us via info@hanatech.community.

And of course: Stay connected! We’re looking forward to seeing you on July 16.

Cheers,

HANA Tech Con Team

entry.title: Re: Implementing "Email Address" in your User Search
entry.content:
@heidirobinson Wonderful!  Glad to hear you are getting value out of it! 

My first guess at the issue would be that some of your "empty" email fields in fact, have white spaces.  Invisible to the eye, but even one space character would make the search selector consider it "not empty".

One way to check this would be to download the search results to CSV, and check it in Notepad (NOT Excel), or, if you have access to the archived connector input files), you could open tthose in NotePad as well.  

 

Excel has a tendancy to modify data, and hide spaces, so its not great for this type of check.

Chris

entry.title: The Architect’s Guide to SAP Fiscal Year Variants: Deciphering the Functional Mechanics of FYV.
entry.content:
Date: February 2026

Author:-  Er. Pratik Das (B.Tech, Mechanical Engineering , SAP Functional Consultant ).

Specialization :- MM, SCM Integration & Business Process Optimization.

Target Audience: SAP Functional Consultants (MM,FI), Solution Architects, Financial Controllers & Business Process Owners (BPO), SAP S/4HANA Migration Leads & etc.

In the SAP ecosystem, the Fiscal Year Variant (FYV) is far more than a simple calendar setting; it is the temporal foundation upon which all transactional integrity rests. For a Material Management (MM) Consultant, the FYV dictates the lifecycle of a Material Document, the calculation of moving average prices, and the strict boundaries of period-end closing via MMPV.

When we configure a system for a global rollout—balancing the Western (K4) and Indian (V3) statutory requirements—precision in OB29 is non-negotiable. Misconfiguration here doesn't just result in a ledger error; it can freeze a warehouse’s ability to post Goods Receipts (GR) or Issue Goods (GI).

  1. The Functional Mechanics of FYV :- 

The Fiscal Year Variant defines the number of posting periods in a fiscal year and the number of special periods. While the standard is 12 posting periods, SAP allows up to 16 periods in total.

  • Posting Periods: These correspond to the operational months where day-to-day procurement and inventory movements occur.
  • Special Periods (13–16): These are strictly for Finance (FI). As an MM consultant, it is vital to remember that logistics movements cannot be posted to special periods. These are reserved for audit adjustments, tax corrections, and year-end closing entries that do not affect physical inventory quantities.
  1. Calendar-Dependent vs. Year-Dependent: The Implementation Choice
  2. Calendar-Dependent (The "Set and Forget" Model)

Used predominantly in Western regions (Standard Variant K4), this configuration assumes the fiscal year is identical to the Gregorian calendar (January 1 to December 31).

  • Consultant Insight: When "Calendar Year" is flagged in OB29, the system ignores the "Period" and "Day" sub-tables. It automatically knows January has 31 days and is Period 01. This is the lowest-maintenance path for a Greenfield implementation.
  1. Year-Dependent (The High-Precision Model)

A variant is "Year-Dependent" if the start and end dates of the periods change every year.

  • The 4-4-5 Retail Cycle: Common in US/UK retail, where months are defined as cycles of weeks to ensure every quarter has exactly 13 weeks. Here, "Month 1" might end on January 28th one year and January 29th the next.
  • Configuration Necessity: Every year, the consultant or the power user must manually maintain the period end-dates for the upcoming year. Failure to do this before the clock strikes midnight on New Year's Eve will result in a global "Period not defined" hard error for every Purchase Order (PO) and GR.
  1. Geographical Logic: Western (K4) vs. Indian (V3)

The primary differentiator in global rollouts is the Year Shift. This is a displacement factor ($+1$, $0$, or $-1$) that aligns the calendar year with the financial year.

The Western Style (K4)

The fiscal year matches the calendar year.

  • January: Period 01 / Shift 0
  • December: Period 12 / Shift 0

The Indian Style (V3)

The Indian fiscal year starts in April. This creates a technical overlap that requires a displacement shift.

  • April to December: These months are part of the current calendar year. Year Shift = 0.
  • January to March: These months belong to the next calendar year but the current fiscal year. To bring them back into the current fiscal year's reporting, we apply a Year Shift = -1.

Consultant Trap: If you are configuring a new Indian Company Code and forget the $-1$ shift for Jan/Feb/March, the system will try to post a March 2026 invoice into Fiscal Year 2026 instead of 2025, throwing the entire Balance Sheet out of equilibrium.

  1. Case Study: Shortened Fiscal Year During Corporate Realignment

The Scenario:

An Indian subsidiary of a German conglomerate is migrating its legacy systems to the global SAP S/4HANA template. The Indian entity currently follows V3 (April–March), but the German parent company mandates K4 (January–December) for global consolidation starting January 1st, 2026.

The Solution: The "Transition Variant"

You cannot simply change the variant in OB37 mid-year; doing so would corrupt the Material Ledger history.

  1. Define the Shortened Year: In OB29, create a new Year-Dependent variant (e.g., Z9).
  2. Period Compression: For the transition year (2025), you define only 9 periods (April 2025 to December 2025).
  3. The Cutover: On December 31st, 2025, the "Year 2025" ends.
  4. The Re-assignment: In the new year, the Company Code is assigned to the standard K4 variant.

Critical MM Checklist for this Transition:

  • MMPV: Ensure the last period of the shortened year (Period 09) is closed correctly.
  • Purchase Orders: Any open POs with delivery dates in the "missing" months (Jan-March) must be reviewed to ensure the new period logic doesn't block the GR.
  • Physical Inventory: A full wall-to-wall count is recommended at the end of the shortened year to "cleanse" the valuation before the new cycle begins.
  1. Integration Points for the MM Consultant

While FI defines the variant, the MM consultant must manage the operational impact:

  • T-Code OMSY: This is where we see the link between the Company Code and the Fiscal Year. It shows the current, previous, and last period of the previous year.
  • T-Code MMPV: This is the "Period Closer." It checks against the FYV to move the "Current Period" forward. If your FYV says Period 12 ends on March 31, but you try to run MMPV on March 30, the system will prevent the shift.
  • Material Valuation: SAP stores material stocks and values by period. If the FYV is changed without a proper migration strategy, the historical stock tables (MARCH, MARDH) will become inconsistent, leading to severe audit failures.

Conclusion

The Fiscal Year Variant is the heartbeat of SAP. Whether you are managing a standard K4 setup or a complex Year-Dependent shortened year for a merger, the logic remains the same: align the calendar to the business cycle through the Year Shift.

entry.title: Re: Custom Logic, BAdi Enhancement Implementation - On Stack Extensibility
entry.content:
Thanks for sharing! It's in the tag but not in the title (maybe would make sense to add it?), so just to clarify: this is for S/4HANA _Public_ edition. 

I doubt information about all the BAdIs for on-prem / Private Cloud edition made it to the API Hub (or if it's even the plan). So if you're not working with a Public Cloud system, you still might need to use the old ways, such as looking in SPRO, good old Google search and setting up debugger in certain class (also googleable information).

Thank you!
